item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is in my opinion the most frightening SCP. If I were to give any Suggestion of how to improve it, I believe Euclid would be a more fitting containment class than Keter. This is because containment is simple, and it appears that the anomaly will not break containment anytime soon. Does anyone have any thoughts about this idea?

item.title: Re: What about SCP 2000?
item.content:encoded: Late response, but I'd say yeah. 2000 must use some sort of AI to locate mission-critical personnel et cetera, so it's probably inert too. That, and/or the BZHRs might become unusable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess was that there are many different dimentions affected by this. The rotten Keller was sent alone through the cave and found a dead world, when he went back he discovered the same thing had happened to his world (the one the logs are set in). Our Keller finds out about this and guesses that if they go back through the cave, the same thing will happen to our world, so he makes sure no one can go back. This could easily be made into a short movie, or even a full length one. Top notch story-telling

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: anti life equation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like it has to be a human, or just something living.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure about that, since Keller wasn't the first one into the cave in our reality, Juno was. Keller and his team went in after. But it is bone-chilling to think that, had the Foundation simply recalled the team to report their findings instead of ordering them to set up camp and continue exploring….well. I don't need to elucidate further.

item.title: Re: Classification?
item.content:encoded: I agree. It should remain keter class because we have no idea what the hell that thing is capable of. It was already a monumentally stupid idea to bring the drone back through, we have no clue how the extinction event happened or why stepping through the cave into one ruined universe and back causes the same thing to happen while sparing the individual. Walling the entrance up may have done nothing. We just don't know. And the Foundation, knowing what's at stake, can't afford to get smug and assume they have the situation neutralized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, yeah, I really liked that detail. Or maybe he was trying to sacrifice himself to "bring back" Dr Bright so he could try and explain just what the hell was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This was an excellent read, probably my new reigning favorite over What Comes Next. Bravo to you, this is excellent. I was genuinely perplexed why Keller set off the on-site nuke, but then the ending came and it all made sense, but not so much so that you know exactly what the anomaly is. Just like a good SCP story should be.

I only have one question that's really been bugging me: If the agents coming back out of the cave would trigger the extinction event, then why was it ok for the drone to pass through?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That buildup was exquisite.
The plot twist is excellent and the delivery just right.

What I really like is how when you get to the end of the last warning you have already figured out why (our) Keller detonated the bomb, but with that last line you see the way that Keller found out about it. Goosebumps.

p.s.
first post here yay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like that…

item.title: What about SCP 2000?
item.content:encoded: Do you guys think SCP-2000 would be rendered inert by this, too? Given that it's just as automated as the other sites, I wonder if it would kick on as designed at some point. The Foundation in this universe wouldn't well ever know, either, considering everything is now blocked off. Thoughts?

item.title: Re: Encrypted message?
item.content:encoded: Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …it also seems that Keller tried suicide-by-Jack-Bright before finally deciding to terminate the team. But it did not work.

item.title: Re: Encrypted message?
item.content:encoded: So Keller just blindly tried a decryption method on the terminal and it worked? And then he passed on the encrypted message for logging and HQ deciphered it but the team was KIA by the time?

item.title: Re: Encrypted message?
item.content:encoded: Keller-1 was able to decrypt the message because Keller-2 used exactly the same cipher that Keller-1 would, because they had been the same person.

item.title: Encrypted message?
item.content:encoded: While the story is really good, I still could not work out how Keller read that security message and acted on it if it was encrypted. Or did the recording equipment cut out because he found some hack to decrypt it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The preservation was ackshually done by CTapbIu_XyJIuraH, I never got around to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: commendations to Gene R and Skodamz for preserving the acrostic in their translations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still come back to this SCP every now and then. Probably the one that got me into the wiki the most and in my Top Three for sure. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a little bit to understand, but once I did, it absolutely shook me to the core. The fact that Keller is pretty much the cause of this each time (that's what I drew from it), and that SCP-2305 simply said "Keller came back" so many times, that probably means that hundreds, if not thousands of universes suffered the same fate. I guess that we just so happened to be the ones who were lucky enough to figure out how to stop it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think that's what it was getting at. I think that it means that all life, whether it's anomalous or non-anomalous, just, well… Stopped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this event kills all life and renders inert all anomalies, do you mean to imply that life itself is anomalous in the Foundation universe, and perhaps even their whole multiverse?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't agree the new changes. I have to zoom out if I want to read all of it before going to the Special Containment Procedures. Definitely, the space used is a lot of problem

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My gut says they might have. Someone in the original reality got that sick feeling in the pit of their stomach and paid attention to it.

They probably saved all life in out universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It can't actively go out and try to kill everything, or enter another reality without help. It requires someone to go into the anomaly and return to their own reality to activate it.

That would be my guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my all-time favorites from my lurking years, and am glad to have re-discovered it now!

One question about the updated classification bar: Why isn't the risk class higher, since this anomaly is capable of killing all life if it can get through? Placing it at "Caution" feels strange for such an absolute eradication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the risk class on this be Critical?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This…this is a special kind of fucked up.

item.title: Re: Classification?
item.content:encoded:
That don't fly like that no more.

A couple of things about this, beyond the notion that I wouldn't know how it flies anymore1;

As to the point of whether or not this article is Keter or otherwise, Mann and Moose made a couple of salient points early on:

Per Mann:

+ show block
– hide block

"But they don't really know, do they? They don't know what happened on the other side of that cave. They don't know if it might happen a second time, and if it might come through. If they knew enough for more extensive procedures, they surely would have them, since this is a phenomenon demonstrably capable of killing all life on Earth.?"

And then, per Moose:

"Agreed. This is Keter. It's Keter because they don't know, and because not knowing could mean the end of all life.

The locked box test isn't appropriate for all situations, but it actually does apply here. They have absolutely no idea if their containment attempt has done anything whatsoever to actually contain the anomaly. They're just guessing.

I mean, concrete? Clearly concrete was meaningless at stopping this anomaly on the other Earth."

The Keter-class is justified due to both the lack of information available on what SCP-2935 is, and also what SCP-2935 has demonstrated it is capable of. They classified it as Keter because they have no clue whether anything they've done to contain SCP-2935 has amounted to anything. If an anomaly is capable of ending all life on Earth, what could possibly be capable of keeping that entity contained?

You say:

Meanwhile, this has been easily contained by… a concrete wall.

-but it hasn't really been contained by a concrete wall. Concrete was a way to keep people out, but it doesn't do anything to affect the anomaly they know exists within the cave. The concrete wall is no different than a fence (and in an earlier draft of this, the eventual concrete barrier was just a fence), and a fence really isn't containment for this.

The thing I take most issue with, however, is your implication that somehow this can't be Keter because the rules for what is Keter have somehow changed, and there is now a hard rule that this article is breaking:

In olden days the inimicity of the object could classify it as Keter, but that was changed.

This is where I stand up from out of my grandpa chair and say as someone who has been here since the days of yore2, there has only ever been one rule about what goes on the wiki - if you can sell it, and people buy it, it goes.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

At the time of this comment's posting, this article has well over +1000 votes and is among the highest rated articles of all time. Forgive me for plucking at my own violin, but I think it's fair to say that this article has been sold and bought enough to justify its internal logic, even if it disrupts the headcanons of a few people.

Footnotes
1. That's a new one.
2. I don't know what inimicity is supposed to mean; that's not a word.

item.title: Re: Classification?
item.content:encoded:
"[…] the fact that an entity was able to kill all sentient life on Earth and we have no idea how it did that is enough to classify it as Keter."
That don't fly like that no more. In olden days the inimicity of the object could classify it as Keter, but that was changed. I quote:

Keter-class SCPs are anomalies that are exceedingly difficult to contain consistently or reliably, with containment procedures often being extensive and complex. The Foundation often can't contain these SCPs well due to not having a solid understanding of the anomaly, or lacking the technology to properly contain or counter it. A Keter SCP does not mean the SCP is dangerous, just that it is simply very difficult or costly to contain.

Lest we forget, SCP-396 is Keter, and it poses very little danger in and of itself. Meanwhile, this has been easily contained by… a concrete wall. Simple and easy. Sure, we don't know for sure if that's all there is to keep it contained (the cornerstone of the Euclid class), but at the same time, it hasn't proven to need any more than that.

item.title: Re: Classification?
item.content:encoded: The access point to the alternate universe itself might be Euclid, but the fact that an entity was able to kill all sentient life on Earth and we have no idea how it did that is enough to classify it as Keter. There was some discussion on the first page, the Foundation isn't even sure if their containment procedures are even doing anything and this entity could very well be able to bypass them.

item.title: Classification?
item.content:encoded: Forgive me if I've skipped something really important, but this one doesn't seem like a true Keter class now that we're judging by necessary containment effort alone. It reads like Euclid at most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author is genius, object is the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The foundation didn't set the nuke. Keller did. Or at least, I'm fairly certain of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Assuming you mean you don’t get why the author ended it with the team being terminated by Site-19’s nuke, it was because of the message posted three days after the event that killed everyone, which alluded that any living thing retiring from the dead universe would cause the universe it entered to spontaneously die, which is also why the MTF were ordered to make a camp, and why the drone was sent to bring samples back. The Foundation likely prevented the death of all things by terminating that MTF squad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
682….dead.

The Hard To Destroy Reptile is dead.

And here we all thought that those words together were impossible.
Good god is this an awesome SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I reckon 682 died because there was nothing left to kill. Like Kuruku-sama from Mahou no Shima e, he survived on hatred, and he died when that source was gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok I know this is old as balls but I haveto reply and explain that it does explain what happens. Here are the clues:
1. Everything is dead
This rules out any deisese or organism such as that. Anything hostile probably didn’t have anything to do with it.
2. It happened the moment they stepped in there
This rules out reality benders or a “monster” like was said by the dude.
3. Keller’s body was in the wrong place and was decomposed.
This means Keller is important somehow, and he survived or died earlier than the others.
4. At some point, Keller decided it was important to nuke the facility.
It’s ridiculous to even consider that they “accidentally” nuked the facility. This indicates that they find something horrific: presumably the recording left behind by the other guy.
5. The recording guy lived as well; this means he must be Keller.
6. Both reality’s died when the previous Keller entered.
Somehow, this cave connects at least 3 realitys, but likely it’s an infinite number of them.
With all this evidence, the signs point to Keller causing the deaths, as at the moment he came in, the world died. If he had gone back to his world, they all would have died, plus the previous Keller killed his world too, so there’s that. Keller nuked the facility to stop the cycle from repeating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty sure nothing actually followed the dude out. Honestly, I think whoever wrote that thing is the real scp. The question is, why did Keller nuke the place? Answer: he’s the one who recorded the message. He had the only out of place corpse, and had a degraded body. He realized that them coming through caused the deaths, and some sort of time-loop was going on. If he went back through, everyone at home would die.
So he nuked the place to stop the time loop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get the ending.

the rest of it favorably reminds me of "Dies the Fire"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Military time doesn't use colons the same way: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/24-hour_clock#Military_time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not add a ":" in the time，like"03:00EST"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the only thing that keeps this from being outright Apollyon class is the fact that we can actually seal off the dang thing.

for this… thing has consumed a thousand universes before it.

our life force is its SUSTENANCE

(sorry if this is a lot of bolded text for one post)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could +1 again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that you took the time and effort to do this is beyond my capacity to understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read the entire april fools edit in a "Goofy" voice.
The experience was much improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hey command, dis is Keller. The, uh… Da on-site nuke at 19 got tripped. We're locked in down 'ere, and… Kael wants yew ter tell Anita what 'e loves 'er, an' Daniels 'as family in Florida, just let 'em know 'e's alright. That yer gon'a be alright. Roy 'as kids, 'e says… Yew get it."
I was fully aware I'd +1'ed previous to what I can only assume is the April Fools edit.
But…
I had to do it again.
This version ought to be a -J in-of-itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 682, Dead? That’s what surprised me the most. I guess he is terminatable. I actually think the lizard would like it there; considering it hates all life…

item.title: Re: Great scip but one thing
item.content:encoded: They have no idea what killed everything on the other side, only anecdotal evidence which implies that testing the posited theory would wipe out all life simultaneously. Since that's an unacceptable result, and it's the only possible way to test its effects, they have their hands tied. "Contained for now" doesn't mean "contained forever." You never know what complications could arise, resulting in somebody walking through to the other side and then heading back. There's a not-zero chance this thing could end up killing the planet and, potentially, the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm wondering if Command suspected something was amiss when they told the first team to wait at the cave entrance instead of going back through…

item.title: Great scip but one thing
item.content:encoded: Keter doesn't make sense because what is designated as 2935? the cave? the alternate reality? the thing that murdered the alternate reality? If its the thing than yea i guess keter could work since they don't know what it is but they still have it contained for now and that should be good enough to classify it as euclid at least or make up a new class since tons of articles have done that. If its the cave or the portal than its definitly safe considering its a Cave under a graveyard that hasn't had a dead body since 1908. The way its worded as "SCP-2935 is a space-time anomaly existing within a limestone cave" So its the portal? if then yea its contained unless someone digs under some random graveyard off the side of the road in Indiana. Maybe making up a new class or just giving it a threat level would make it work but eh im not that good of a writer so what do I know. Other than the object class nitpick I still think its a great SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All our times have come
Here but now they're gone
Seasons don't fear the reaper
Nor do the wind, the sun or the rain, we can be like they are

One of my favorite skips. +1

item.title: Re: +1000
item.content:encoded: Yeah
cool

item.title: Mild confusion with a small theory
item.content:encoded:
Writing this from my phone.
I think the main piece of this work was declaring life itself as some form of anomaly. I know it sounds nonsensical, but hear me out.
Every skip is dead, and powerless. Anomalous objects just don't work, and all complex creatures have ceased their function.
I'm not really sure what caused it, I was disappointed to see that it wasn't clear. I'm not really sure what was going on with Keller/the nuke either.

item.title: Does this cave actually exist?
item.content:encoded: I live very close to Joppa and I think it would be cool to go see this cave (if it exists, of course!)

item.title: The fuck did ya do to the CSS??
item.content:encoded: Why are you like this — why are you such a magnificent human being?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something here destroyed absolutely everything that an intelligent viewpoint would describe as "life," and left absolutely no collateral damage. That doesn't sound like an unthinking force of nature to me.

And I stated that "life" as a metaphorical concept was erased or destroyed. Of course, if the universe suddenly stopped being able to support the metaphysical thing called "life", even for a moment, it would appear exactly as we saw in the SCP. Machines work, AI don't. Oils and other biological byproducts are still present, but metabolic reactions don't occur. A statue made of rebar and concrete still works, but it remains immobile. The result is something that appears to be very clean, very targeted, and looks much like an intelligence was behind it.

So we can assume that in all likelihood that his "it followed me" idea is correct. Which, again, implies the presence of some intelligence behind the events.

So, why didn't it follow the drone out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, one man's ravings do not make it true. That would be my response to the idea that he himself was the cause. Survivor guilt had driven him near mad, most likely. I don't support the "some entity did this" idea just because the log hypothesized it, I support it because the evidence supports the hypothesis. If this phenomenon had wiped out only organic life, it'd be one thing. Even if it wiped out synthetic life while also frying all other forms of electronics. But it didn't, it picked and chose.

What's alive and what's not is as much a philosophical question as it is a scientific one. Even here, in real life, with only naturally occurring organic life on one planet, scientists still debate over whether viruses are alive. In a universe like SCP, the question becomes exponentially more difficult to answer. Something here destroyed absolutely everything that an intelligent viewpoint would describe as "life," and left absolutely no collateral damage. That doesn't sound like an unthinking force of nature to me.

Furthermore, we have to consider that this man did explore another dead universe and the phenomenon somehow followed him back to his own universe. There's no indication that he was delusional, and the state of his body (hell, just the fact that he knows what happened and didn't drop dead the same time as everyone else) supports his story. So, we know that "it" got out, but did it really follow him or was that just more survivor guilt? We don't have a lot to go on, but I believe that yes, it did indeed follow him. The reason is, the main SCP universe was obviously not hit with this phenomenon. They got signals in and out, they sent a team in, they even extracted some samples. The only difference is that a living person did not come back to the main universe from the dead one. So we can assume that in all likelihood that his "it followed me" idea is correct. Which, again, implies the presence of some intelligence behind the events.

One thing I do wonder is whether the entire universe was wiped out, or just Earth. It would be near-impossible to find out though, even if personnel were sent back in, due to the limitations of light speed.
